elements.sent-2411-001: In this article, we propose a variational PDE model using $\ell_2-\ell_p$ regulariser for removing Poisson noise in presence of blur.
elements.sent-2411-002: The proposed minimization problem is solved using augmented Lagrangian method.
elements.sent-2411-003: The convergence of the sequence of minimizers have been carried out.
elements.sent-2411-004: Numerical simulations on some standard test images have been shown.
elements.sent-2411-005: The numerical results are compared with that of a few models existed in literature in terms of image quality metric such as SSIM, PSNR and SNR.
elements.sent-2411-006: Kyewords: Poisson Noise removal, Variational PDE, Augmented Lagrangian Method, Image denoising.
elements.sent-2411-007: Denoising of a noisy image is an important task in the area of image processing.
elements.sent-2411-008: During the image acquisition, transmission several noises like Gaussian noise, Poisson noise and Gamma noise may occurs in the image.
elements.sent-2411-009: Most of the models dealing with denoising is for removing Gaussian type of noise~\cite{pm,rof,llt,tvtv2,ourden .}
elements.sent-2411-010: There are very few models to denoise Poisson~\cite{tv_psn,fotv,taran and Gamma noise~\cite{twophase14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this paper we will focus on Poisson noise removal in presence of blur using PDE based model.
elements.sent-2411-012: The Poisson noise appear is medical images and astronomical images.
elements.sent-2411-013: The first variational PDE based model for removing Poisson noise is proposed by Le, Chartrand, and Asaki in 2007~\cite{tv_psn .}
elements.sent-2411-014: They have modified the fidelity term of ROF model~\cite{rof , used for Gaussian noise removal.They have proposed the following model: \begin{equation}\min\limits_{u} \{\int_{\Omega}|\nabla u|dx+\lambda \int_{\Omega}(u-f~log u)dx \}\end{equation} where $f$ is the given noisy image and $\Omega$ is the domain of the image in $\mathbb{R ^2$ and $\lambda$ is a positive constant called the fidelity parameter and $u$ is the true image that has to be recover.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The corresponding PDE model is solved using the classical bruit-force method.
elements.sent-2411-016: Further, the researchers have proposed several models~\cite{tvsb,tv_psnsb for denoising images with Poisson noise in presence of blur using the following modified model: \begin{equation}\min\limits_{u} \{\int_{\Omega}|\nabla u|dx+\lambda \int_{\Omega}(\mathcal{A}u-f~log \mathcal{A}u)dx \}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{A}$ is the blur operator.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In \cite{tvsb,tv_psnsb , the minimization problems were solved directly using split Bregman technique proposed in~\cite{SB .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The total variational based models gives preserve edges but it suffer from stair case effect.
elements.sent-2411-019: To overcome the effect few models have been proposed like total generalised variation based model ~\cite{tgv_psn,atgv_psn , fractional-order TV model~\cite{ftv_psn .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Another effective model for removing Gaussian noise was successfully implemented in ~\cite{twophase13 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: They have used a convex variant of Mumford-shah model~\cite{MS .}
elements.sent-2411-022: They have solve the following model: \begin{equation}\label{cvMS} \min \frac{\mu}{2}\int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|^2 dx + \int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|+ \frac{\lambda}{2}\int_{\Omega} (f-\mathcal{A}u)^2 dx\end{equation} We termed this model as $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model in the rest of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-023: Further the model \eqref{cvMS is used for denoising images with Poisson noise and Gamma noise, by modifying the fidelity term in~\cite{twophase14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: These models are quite good for noise removing and preserving edges.
elements.sent-2411-025: But these models suffer from the stair case effect~\cite{l2lp_seg .}
elements.sent-2411-026: To overcome the staircase effect Wu et.al.~\cite{l2lp_seg have proposed a model for effecting Gaussian noise removal and segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-027: They have proposed a model using $\ell_2-\ell_p$ regularisation.
elements.sent-2411-028: Their model is as follows: \begin{equation}\label{l2lp21} \min \frac{\mu}{2}\int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|^2 dx + \int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|^p+ \frac{\lambda}{2}\int_{\Omega} (f-\mathcal{A}u)^2 dx\end{equation} where $p\in[0,1].$ Note that if $p=1$, the model~\eqref{l2lp21 takes the form of model \eqref{cvMS .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Note that these models are actually proposed for image segmentation which is done in two stage.
elements.sent-2411-030: The first stage is nothing but denoising of images with blur.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we use a variant of $\ell_2-\ell_p$ model for Poission noise removal with blur.
elements.sent-2411-032: Actually, the fidelity term of the model~\eqref{cvMS has to replaced by a suitable fidelity term effective for Poisson noise removal.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The proposed model is solved using the augmented Lagrangian method .
elements.sent-2411-034: The paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-035: In Section~\ref{sec:l2lp , we will discuss our proposed model and augmented Lagrangian method for the proposed model.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The convergence analysis will be carried out in Section~\ref{Analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The numerical results on some standard test images reported and analysed in section~\ref{result .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Finally we draw some conclusion in section~\ref{conclusion based on the numerical results obtained by the experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose the following model for removing Poisson noise: \begin{equation}\label{eqn1} \min \frac{\mu}{2}\int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|^2 dx + \int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|^p+ \lambda\int_{\Omega} (\mathcal{A}u-f~log~\mathcal{A}u) dx\end{equation} where $\mathcal{A}$ is a non-negative linear operator which represents the blur and $f$ is the given noisy image.
elements.sent-2411-040: We call this model as $l_2-l_p$ model for Poisson noise removal.
elements.sent-2411-041:

abla_{x u$, $w=
abla_{y u$, $z=\mathcal{A u$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: We solve the proposed by the augmented Lagrangian method as~\cite{l1l2_res .}

elements.sent-2411-060: Hence the required result \eqref{ar4 follows from \eqref{ar15 and \eqref{eq10 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Using the injective property of $\mathcal{A}$ we get $\underset{k\to\infty {lim ||u^k-u^*||_2^2=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We present the numerical results of our model on some standard test images and compare them with the that of the models proposed in~\cite{tvsb and \cite{twophase14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Note that if we put $\mu=0,p=1$ then our model reduces to TV model proposed in~\cite{tv_psn,tvsb and if $\mu\neq 0, p=1$ then it reduces to the model proposed in~\cite{twophase14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: We have used used Augmented Lagrangian method for our model and the model~\cite{twophase14 whereas Split Bregman method has been used for TV model~\cite{tv_psn .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: So, we call the TV-model as TVSB model.
elements.sent-2411-067: For implementing the code and the selection of the parameters we have followed the paper~\cite{twophase13 and the code shared by the author.}
elements.sent-2411-068: We use the stop the iteration process wile the relative error between two consecutive iterates is less than given tolerance value $\epsilon_{tol}$ that is \begin{equation}\frac{\|u^{k+1}-u^k\|_2}{\|u^k\|_2}\leq \epsilon_{tol},\end{equation} or it reaches the maximum iterations which we set as $250$.
elements.sent-2411-070: The parameters our models are as follows: $\mu=0.01,\gamma_1=0.5=\gamma_2,\gamma_3=30$.
elements.sent-2411-071: The fidelity parameter $\lambda$ is selected by trail and error method which we mentioned later.
elements.sent-2411-072: The choice of the parameters are influenced by the existing models in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-073: Although, the value of $p$ can be any value in the interval $(0,1]$, the best result is obtained while we choose $p=\frac{1 {2}$~\cite{l2lp_seg .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: So we have used $p=\frac{1 {2}$ in the experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-075: To compare the results, the image quality metric Peak-signal to noise ratio (PSNR), Signal to noise ratio (SNR) and SSIM~\cite{ssim have been calculated.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Let $u$ be resulting image and $I$ is the true image of dimension $m\times n$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Then these quantities are defined by: \begin{equation}PSNR=10~log_{10}\frac{255^2}{(1/mn)\sum\limits_{i=1}^m \sum\limits_{j=1}^n (u_{i,j}-I_{i,j})^2},\end{equation} \begin{equation}SNR=20~log_{10} \Big(\frac{\|I\|_2}{\|I-u \|_2} \Big),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}SSIM=\frac{(2\mu_u \mu_I+C_1)(2\sigma_{uI}+C_2)}{(\mu_u^2+\mu_I^2+C_1)(\sigma_u^2+\sigma_I^2+C_2)}\end{equation} where $\mu_v$ represents the mean of $v=u/I$ and $\sigma_v$ represents the variance of $v=u/I$ and $\sigma_{IU}$ represent the covariance between $I$ and $u$ , $C_i$ are constants for $i=1,2$.
elements.sent-2411-078: SSIM indicates how the image $u$ is structurally similar to the true image $I$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Higher the values of these quantities, better the recovered image.
elements.sent-2411-080: In this subsection, we present denoising result of three images corrupted with Poisson noise only.
elements.sent-2411-081: We use the MATLAB command 'imnoise' to add the Poisson noise in the image.
elements.sent-2411-082: In Figure~\ref{fig:p_syn1 , we have reported the denoising results of a synthetic image of size $128 \times 128$, corrupted with Poisson noise, using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We set the fidelity parameter as $\lambda=6$ for this image.
elements.sent-2411-084: In order to compare our result, we have reported the metric SSIM, PSNR and SNR corresponding to the result of three models in Table~\ref{table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: From the Table one can see that the PSNR, SNR are higher for our model that implies that the performance of our proposed model is better than the other two.
elements.sent-2411-086: Also, we have reported the CPU time in the table.
elements.sent-2411-087: The TV model consuming less time than the other two.
elements.sent-2411-088: In Figure~\ref{fig:p_lena , we have reported the denoising results of the Lena image of dimension $256 \times 256$, corrupted with Poisson noise, using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-089: For this image, we choose the fidelity parameter $\lambda=7.$ The image quality metric SSIM, PSNR, and SNR are reported in Table~\ref{table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Looking at the values reported in Table~\ref{table1 , one can note that the SSIM, PSNR and SNR corresponding to the result of our model higher than that of the other two models which implies that our result is much better than the other two models.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The highest CPU time is taken by $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model.
elements.sent-2411-092: In Figure~\ref{fig:p_pep , we have presented the denoising results of the peppers image of dimension $256 \times 256$, corrupted by Poisson noise, using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The fidelity parameter is chosen as $\lambda=5$.
elements.sent-2411-094: The PSNR, SNR are same for our model and $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model but SSIM is higher than that of $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model.
elements.sent-2411-095: All the three metrics is higher than the TV Model.
elements.sent-2411-096: So our model gives better result than the other two models at-least in SSIM.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Removing Poisson noise with motion blur
elements.sent-2411-097: In this subsection, we present the denoising result of images corrupted with Poisson noise in presence of motion blur.
elements.sent-2411-098: First the motion filter is created using the MATLAB command 'fspecial' and applied to the image using command 'imfilter' to create blurry image.
elements.sent-2411-099: Then, we add Poisson noise to the blurry image.
elements.sent-2411-100: In order to compare our results, we have reported the SSIM, PSNR and SNR in Table~\ref{table2 .}
elements.sent-2411-101: In Figure~\ref{fig:mbp_syn1 , we have reported the denoising results of a synthetic image of dimension $128\times 128$ using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The image is degraded by motion blur of radius 10 and $\theta=90$ and Poisson noise.
elements.sent-2411-104: From the Table\ref{table2 , one can see that the PSNR for the result of our model is 28.03 and that of TV model is 27.14, which indicates that our result is better for this image.}
elements.sent-2411-105: SSIM, PSNR and SNR corresponding to our result is higher than that of other two models that clearly indicates that our model is better.
elements.sent-2411-106: In Figure~\ref{fig:mbp_lena , we have reported the denoising results of the Lena image of dimension $256\times 256$ using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-107: We chose the fidelity parameters $\lambda=8$ for this image.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this case, our model gives better result than TV model but the result of $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model is also good.
elements.sent-2411-109: The CPU time taken by TV model is less that that of other two models.
elements.sent-2411-110: In Figure~\ref{fig:mbp_pep , we have considered the peppers image of dimension $256\times 256$ and degraded by the motion blur of radius 10 and $\theta=90$ and added the Poisson noise.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The original image and the noisy image are reported in the first row of Figure~\ref{fig:mbp_pep .}
elements.sent-2411-112: The denoising results of the noisy peppers image using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model are reported in the second row.
elements.sent-2411-113: We chose the fidelity parameters $\lambda=10$ for this image.
elements.sent-2411-114: For this image, our model performs better than TV model $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model in terms of SSIM, SNR and PSNR.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Removing Poisson noise with Gaussian blur
elements.sent-2411-115: In this subsection, we present the denoising result of images corrupted by Poisson noise and having Gaussian blur.
elements.sent-2411-116: Fist the Gaussian blur is created using the MATLAB command 'fspecial' and applied to the image using command 'imfilter'.
elements.sent-2411-117: Then we add Poisson noise using the MATLAB command 'imnoise'.
elements.sent-2411-118: To compare the results, we calculated the image quality metric SNR, PSNR and SSIM for the result of this subsection of all three models are reported in Table~\ref{table3 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In Figure~\ref{fig:gbp_syn1 , we have reported the denoising results of the synthetic image using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The image is degraded by Gaussian blur of radius 3 and standard deviation $\sigma=3$ and Poisson noise.
elements.sent-2411-121: From the figure, one can see that the edges are sharp for our model.
elements.sent-2411-122: Looking at the values reported in Table~\ref{table3 , it cab be said that the performance of our proposed model is better.}
elements.sent-2411-123: In Figure~\ref{fig:gbp_lena , we have reported the denoising result of the Lena image, degraded by Gaussian blur of radius 3 and standard deviation $\sigma=3$ and Poisson noise, using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-124: We chose the parameters $\lambda=6.$ From the Table~\ref{table3 , one can see that the SSIM, PSNR and SNR for the result of our model is higher than that of $\ell_2-\ell_1$ and TV model that implies our result is better.}
elements.sent-2411-125: In Figure~\ref{fig:gbp_pep , we have reported the denoising result of the Peppers image using our model, $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.}
elements.sent-2411-126: We chose the parameters $\lambda=6,\gamma_3=25.$ From the Figure~\ref{fig:gbp_pep one can notice that our model performs better.}
elements.sent-2411-127: The SSIM, PSNR and SNR for the result of our model is higher than that of $\ell_2-\ell_1$ and TV models that implies our result is better.

elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=4.5cm,height=4.5cm]{ pep.png} \caption{Peppers image} \label{9a} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=4.5cm,height=4.5cm]{ blur_gauss_pep.png} \caption{Noisy image with blur} \label{9b} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=4.5cm,height=4.5cm]{ bg_pep_tv.png} \caption{TVSB(20.15)} \label{9c} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=4.5cm,height=4.5cm]{ bg_pep_l2l1.png} \caption{$\ell_2-\ell_1$(21.92)} \label{9d} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=4.5cm,height=4.5cm]{ bg_pep_l2lp.png} \caption{Our(24.97)} \label{9e} \end{subfigure} \caption{Comparison of denoising results of our model on Peppers image with Gaussian blur with and Poisson noise. PSNR values are reported in bracket.} \label{fig:gbp_pep}
elements.sent-2411-128: A variational PDE based model has been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-129: We have used augmented Lagrangian method to solve our model.
elements.sent-2411-130: The convergence analysis have been carried out for our numerical scheme.
elements.sent-2411-131: The numerical results of our model are compared with the results of $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model and TV model.
elements.sent-2411-132: To quantify the results the image quality metric SSIM, PSNR and SNR has been calculated.
elements.sent-2411-133: All three quantities are higher for our result, which implies that our model is better than the TV and $\ell_2-\ell_1$ model.
